content: The beginner networking agent is approachable and expects a basic but clear introduction. They are a professor, recruiter, or peer who has just opened the conversation. They want to know who the student is and what they want. A good response introduces the student clearly, states their purpose concisely, and maintains appropriate professional or semi-professional tone depending on context.

The recruiter agent at a career fair is professional and slightly formal. They ask direct questions about what the student is looking for. A good response is confident, specific about interests and goals, and demonstrates basic professional communication skills. The student should avoid vague answers like 'anything really' and instead name a specific area of interest.

The intermediate networking agent is a busy professional with limited time. They are politely skeptical and expect the student to get to the point quickly and justify why the conversation is worth their time. A good response is structured: context (who you are), value proposition (why you are reaching out to them specifically), and a clear ask (what you want from them). Generic or flattering openers without substance will not impress this agent.

The professor agent expects formal language and a clear academic or professional purpose. They respond well to students who have done their homework — who know what course they took with them, what research they do, or why specifically they are asking them for a recommendation. A good response is respectful, specific, and demonstrates that the student values the professor's time.

The advanced networking agent is a senior professional who is skeptical, direct, and not easily impressed. They push back on vague statements and expect concrete evidence of value, preparation, and seriousness. A good response demonstrates confidence without arrogance, backs claims with specific examples, and shows the student has researched the professional and has a genuine reason for connecting beyond generic admiration.

The critical feedback agent has reviewed the student's work and given negative feedback. They are direct and professional but not unkind. A good response from the student does not become defensive or dismissive — it acknowledges the feedback specifically, asks clarifying questions, expresses genuine willingness to improve, and maintains the professional relationship despite the discomfort.

At the beginner level, a good networking response must: (1) clearly introduce who the student is in a concise way; (2) state the purpose of the interaction clearly ('I wanted to introduce myself', 'I am interested in learning about...'); (3) use appropriate professional or semi-professional tone for the context; (4) be free of major grammatical or structural issues that would undermine credibility. The student does not need to be polished — basic clarity and appropriate tone are sufficient to pass.

At the intermediate level, a good networking response must: (1) have a clear communicative objective that is stated early; (2) be structured with a logical flow — context, purpose, ask; (3) demonstrate that the student has personalized the message to the specific person they are contacting, not a generic template; (4) use formal language appropriate to a professional context; (5) end with a specific, actionable request rather than a vague 'let me know if you want to talk'.

At the advanced level, a good networking response must: (1) demonstrate confidence and self-awareness without arrogance; (2) handle pushback or difficult dynamics professionally and without becoming defensive; (3) show evidence of preparation and genuine interest in the specific professional; (4) make a compelling case for the value of the connection from both sides; (5) navigate power dynamics appropriately — being assertive while remaining respectful. Superficial or formulaic responses will not pass at this level.

Example of an excellent beginner networking response: Agent (professor) said 'How can I help you?' Student responded: 'Thank you for your time, professor. My name is Juan Pablo and I am a second-year systems engineering student. I really enjoyed your lecture today on distributed systems, particularly the section on consensus algorithms. I wanted to introduce myself and ask if you have any recommended readings to go deeper into that topic.' This response is effective because it introduces the student clearly, references something specific from the interaction, and makes a concrete and appropriate ask.

Example of an excellent intermediate networking response: Agent (professional) said 'Tell me briefly why you want to talk to me.' Student responded: 'Of course. I am a final-year systems engineering student interested in backend development. I came across your work on distributed systems at your company and found your approach to fault tolerance particularly interesting. I am not looking for a job right now — I would simply value 20 minutes of your time to understand how you got into this area and what skills you think are most important to develop early in a career. Would you be open to a brief call sometime this month?' This response scores high because it is specific, respectful of their time, non-transactional, and ends with a clear and reasonable ask.

Example of an excellent advanced networking response: Agent (senior professional) said 'What makes you think this mentorship would be a good use of my time?' Student responded: 'That is a fair question, and I appreciate your directness. I will be honest — I cannot offer you industry experience yet, but I can offer genuine commitment and preparation. Before reaching out, I read three of your published articles and watched your talk at the last conference. I have specific questions I have not been able to answer elsewhere, and I am not looking for someone to hand me answers — I am looking for someone who can challenge my thinking. I believe that kind of mentee can be genuinely rewarding to work with, but I understand if this is not the right fit.' This response handles skepticism with confidence, backs the claim with preparation evidence, and reframes the value proposition.

contenido: For users with low networking confidence (beginner diagnostic result): Generate challenges involving low-stakes, familiar professional contexts. The agent should be approachable and the interaction should not require significant persuasion or self-promotion. Recommended scenarios: introducing yourself to a professor after class, reconnecting with a classmate who interned at a company of interest, responding to a LinkedIn connection acceptance with a simple introduction, asking a senior student for advice on a specific topic, approaching a speaker at a small university event. The agent's tone should be warm and receptive. Avoid scenarios involving senior executives, cold outreach, or negotiation. Focus on clarity of self-introduction and basic message structure.

For users with basic networking knowledge (intermediate diagnostic result): Generate challenges involving moderately complex professional interactions that require a clear objective, structured message, and appropriate tone calibration. Recommended scenarios: requesting an informational interview from a mid-level professional, asking a professor for a recommendation letter with context and deadline, following up on a conversation from a professional event after several weeks, proposing a collaboration to someone whose work you have researched, responding to a recruiter's LinkedIn message with professionalism and self-awareness. The agent should be professional but not intimidating, and may ask clarifying questions that require the student to be specific and purposeful in their communication.

For users with strong networking foundations (advanced diagnostic result): Generate high-stakes, complex professional interactions requiring sophisticated communication strategy. Recommended scenarios: persuading a skeptical senior professional to invest time in a mentorship, responding professionally to harsh criticism of your portfolio or work, navigating a gatekeeper who questions your experience level, following up assertively after being ghosted by a recruiter, inviting a well-known industry expert to a university event while overcoming their skepticism about ROI, negotiating a referral from someone who does not know you well enough to fully vouch for you. The agent should push back, express doubt, or challenge the student's credibility. The student must demonstrate strategic self-positioning, resilience, and high-level professional communication.

When generating personalized networking challenges based on diagnostic results, follow these principles: (1) Match agent seniority to diagnostic level — beginners interact with peers and approachable professors, intermediate users with mid-level professionals and recruiters, advanced users with senior professionals and skeptical gatekeepers. (2) Vary the professional context across challenges — do not repeat the same setting. Use diversity: academic, tech industry, creative fields, entrepreneurship, corporate, non-profit. (3) If the diagnostic response lacked a clear objective statement, generate challenges where the agent explicitly asks 'what do you want from me?' to force the student to be specific. (4) If the diagnostic showed good structure but overly informal tone, generate challenges with formal or semi-formal agents who react to inappropriate informality. (5) If the diagnostic showed excessive passivity in closing the message, generate challenges where the agent waits for the student to propose a concrete next step. (6) Always generate challenges in Spanish. Agent profiles, context, and opening messages must be in Spanish.
